subject: Simple weapons (clubs, maces, sickles, javelins, handaxes, light hammers, torches)
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 063 (not the heuristic)
note: All 39 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content. Rated by power when acquired. Note auto-caption renderings: Loviatar's Scourge='levar's Scourge', The Blood of Lathander='Blood of Leander', Deva Mace='Daa mace', Xyanyde='Zionite', Sickle of Boooal='sickle of Bal'.

Loviatar's Scourge.why: A no-enhancement mace dealing 2d6 (the highest-damage simple weapon for a long while) plus +1d6 necrotic AOE to everything in melee on each hit, with necrotic resistance offsetting the self-damage; an early-game power spike, barterable/pickpocketable in the goblin camp.

Shattered Flail.why: A +2 mace (very strong this early) that heals 1d6 on every hit — incredible free sustain, especially with cleave/multi-target attacks and on-heal synergies; the catch is a madness debuff if you go a turn without hitting, easily managed with Wisdom-save gear.

The Blood of Lathander.why: A legendary +3 mace that passively blinds nearby undead/fiends (a condition, so it stacks radiating orb), revives you once/rest, and casts level-6 Sunbeam once/rest — a level-6 spell in Act 1; any one of its effects would justify S.

Deva Mace.why: Glitch-only, but the highest-damage weapon in the game: d6 + 4d8 radiant on every hit, one-handed (dual-wieldable); not intended to be obtainable, but if you exploit it in you get absurd, build-warping damage output.

Devotee's Mace.why: A legendary +3 mace with +1d8 radiant (great for radiating-orb clerics) and a once/rest Healing Incense aura (party-wide 1d4/turn for 10 turns that triggers on-heal items) — pairs with Blood of Lathander (cast Sunbeam, then swap to this).

+1 Javelin.why: A magical thrown weapon with one of the highest thrown damage dice; throwing-weapon characters want as many magical throwables as possible (their main early damage before a returning weapon), so stockpile these.

+1 Handaxe.why: Same value as the +1 Javelin — a high-damage-die magical thrown weapon to stockpile for throwers (and any melee character wanting a ranged option); the +N adds to thrown damage even though not to the thrown attack roll.

Club of Hill Giant Strength.why: A plain club that sets Strength to 19 — competes with the (stronger) Giant Strength elixirs, but it's great as an off-hand stat stick for a Tavern Brawler monk or on a Titanstring archer, freeing the elixir slot for Bloodlust/Vigilance/etc.

Ritual Axe.why: A no-enhancement handaxe that applies Bane (-1d4 to enemy attacks AND saves) with NO save on every hit — a powerful, condition-counting debuff that sets up control spells; you take 1d6 on hit above 50% HP, mitigable with resistance.

Shining Staver-of-Skulls.why: A +1 light hammer with +1d4 radiant and a light radius; a great throwable to keep on hand (extra damage die, applies radiating orb) and a bound-weapon option for an Eldritch Knight — most parties should carry it.

Xyanyde.why: A +1 mace whose once/rest effect, on a MISS, applies Faerie Fire with no save — granting your whole party advantage against that enemy for two turns; a uniquely reliable (miss-triggered, no-save) effect, great swapped in for single-boss fights.

Shadow Lantern.why: A story club (poor for attacking) that conjures a Shadow once/rest — a strong summon (high damage, strength-drain) for the cost of holding the weapon; great on characters who don't need their weapon slot, swap away if it dies.

Handaxe.why: A light, thrown d6 — the highest light-weapon damage die, so dual-wieldable, but mainly valued (with daggers/javelins) as a thrown-weapon stockpile, including to open combat without losing your returning weapon to the recall bug.

Javelin.why: A thrown d6 with Piercing Strike; same stockpile value as the handaxe for throwers and as a backup ranged option for any party member (you rarely activate its melee weapon action since you're throwing it).

+1 Mace.why: A real upgrade over the base mace for a shield-using simple-weapon character (e.g. cleric) who actually makes weapon attacks; worth it only if they swing often, otherwise skippable.

Dragon's Grasp.why: A no-enhancement handaxe with +1d4 vs burning enemies; the situational extra die is nice in a fire-themed party that burns enemies regularly, but most parties get more from a plain +1 handaxe's accuracy.

Torch.why: Lit, it deals d4 + d4 fire — the best one-handed damage from the very start — plus it lights areas and ignites surfaces, and works with Shillelagh; all duplicable (fire-dip any weapon, Light cantrip), but a handy all-in-one to carry if you don't micro-dip.

Ironwood Club.why: A +1 club that adds +1d4 when Shillelagh'd (d8 + 1d4 + fire-dip) and grants Back Breaker (prone); a solid damage option for a Shillelagh nature cleric/Druid or dual-wielding Spore Druid, though many prefer a magical staff.

Wavemother's Sickle.why: A +2 sickle with +1d4 cold and advantage vs Wet enemies; decent damage that doubles via cold vulnerability, a reasonable dual-wield pick for a Spore Druid without shortsword proficiency.

Mace.why: The non-magical d6 mace with Concussive Smash; the best simple one-hander for an early shield-user with only simple proficiency, but you'll never equip a plain mace later — and may prefer a spear/quarterstaff anyway.

Adamantine Mace.why: A +1 mace that auto-crits objects and makes ALL your bludgeoning damage ignore resistance — useless as a weapon, but a niche off-hand stat stick for a monk's unarmed strikes; costs an adamantine forge (better spent on armor).

Redcap Sickle.why: A no-enhancement sickle dealing 2d4 (fire-dippable); a touch more base damage than other early weapons, but most characters prefer the accuracy/enhancement of a +1 weapon (especially Spore Druids landing their passive damage).

Salami.why: Mechanically just a club, BUT it's both a weapon and a Camp Supply — so a Chimpanzee-aspect Barbarian throwing it blinds enemies, and an Eldritch Knight can bind it for a returning blind-thrower ('Salami Slinger'); a real, if goofy, niche build.

Sickle of Boooal.why: A 2d4 sickle with advantage vs bleeding enemies; setting up bleed usually costs an action and the base weapon is weak by the time you get it, so it's mainly relevant as the source of its permanent story buff.

Comeback Handaxe.why: A joke +1 returning handaxe that hits YOU on return and goes to inventory (not re-equipping) — uniquely, that lets you throw it while keeping two passive stat-stick weapons equipped (e.g. Club of Hill Giant Strength); niche but real.

Handmaiden's Mace.why: A +2 mace with +1d6 poison that sets Strength to 18; redundant with the Club of Hill Giant Strength / elixirs, and arrives late when +2 + poison is unexciting (many Act 3 enemies are poison-immune) — only for no-elixir or poison-synergy builds.

Club.why: A light d4 with Concussive Smash, but strictly outclassed — dual-wield handaxes (d6) or use a mace instead; the base club never sees play.

Light Hammer.why: A light, thrown d4 — strictly better than a club (can throw it), but its low die and arcing throw make handaxes/javelins/daggers better throwables, so no reason to use it.

Sickle.why: A light d4 with the weak Lacerate action — effectively a worse club/light hammer (already D), so unsurprisingly D too.

+1 Club.why: A generic +1 club; still outclassed by handaxes/maces, and the +1 to hit isn't worth the money over carrying a thrown weapon — safely ignored.

+1 Light Hammer.why: Since the enhancement doesn't apply to thrown attack rolls, it throws like a plain handaxe/javelin while being a worse melee weapon — same D tier as the +1 club.

Ceremonial Mace.why: A +1 mace used in a story event; by the time you get it you have far better options and could've bought a +1 mace long ago — D for weapon use.

Skybreaker.why: A +1 light hammer with Searing Smite once/rest; you give up too much damage for one fire-Smite a day, and a scroll/grenade does burst better — leave it home.

Sussur Sickle.why: A +1 sickle that silences on a DC12 con save — fine, but it's the worst of the three Sussur weapons: the dagger is strictly better (finesse, better action) and the greatsword hits harder, so there's no reason to pick the sickle.

Moonlantern.why: A story-event club with no magical effect; useful only for the story, not as a weapon.

Wulbren's Hammer.why: A +1 light hammer with +2d4 force vs objects only; breaking doors faster isn't a meaningful combat effect, so it's low-damage and skippable.

+2 Mace.why: A generic +2 mace found only late; you'll already have a +3 legendary mace, so there's no reason to use it.

Infernal Mace.why: A +2 mace with a flat 3 poison on hit — duplicated and bettered by the Handmaiden's Mace (1d6 + strength set), and far too little damage for Act 3.

Torch of Revocation.why: A +1 torch adding necrotic + fire (3d4+1 lit) — a fun two-element idea, but you can replicate it by fire-dipping a better weapon, and the damage is unexciting for most Act 3 characters.
